---
title: "Blog Post"
format: html
author: "Jay Kim, William Lien, Ava Robillard"
date: 3/5/26
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: inline
---

# **Does urban greening reduce crime and promote health?**

### A replication of analysis from A Difference-in-Differences Analysis of Health, Safety, and Greening Vacant Urban Space (Branas et al., 2011)


The Pennsylvania Horticultural Society (PHS) directs a program to clean, green and maintain abandoned lots in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. The authors of this study conducted a decade-long difference-in-differences analysis of the impact of the PHS vacant lot greening program on health and safety outcomes.

In this blog post, we will replicate this study with simulated data to dive further into the causal claims.

```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
# 
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
library(fixest)
library(gtsummary)
library(janitor)
library(stringr)
library(gt)
library(broom)
```

### Read in data

```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Read in simulated data
sim_data <- read_csv(here("data", "branas2011_simulated_panel.csv")) %>%
  # Drop simulation-only helper variables
  select(-ends_with("_z")) %>% 
  select(-cov_part)
```

---------------------------------------

### 1. Data Description

#### Key variables and data structure

In this data, **each row** represents a vacant lot x year observation (i.e. L00001 x 2008). The **unit of analysis** of this study is vacant lot x year.

The **treatment variable** represented vacant lots greened by the PHS program, during which there was a consistent treatment protocol of removing trash and debris, grading the land, planting grass and trees to create a park-like setting, and installing low wooden posts around each lot's perimeter to show that this lot was cared for. PHS returned multiple times each year for basic maintenance.

The **control variable** represented control lots from 2 eligibility pools were randomly selected and matched to treated lots at a 3:1 ratio by city section - vacant lots eligible to serve as controls included only those that had never been greened from 1999 to 2008 but could have been chosen by the PHS for greening.

The **outcome variables** were various health and safety outcomes. Health outcomes include high stress, high cholesterol, high blood pressure, low exercise, poor health status. Safety outcomes include gun assaults, robberies, disorderly, and illegal dumping.

Overall, the difference-in-differences approach considers these health and safety outcomes occurring on and around vacant lots in the PHS program before and after they were treated, as compared with matched control vacant lots over the same time period.


#### Notable data cleaning decisions 

'Point-based' models where crimes (geocoded events) and health outcomes (survey-based) are converted into per-lot measurements using spatial methods (kernel density, inverse-distance weighting) and then modeled in DID regressions- in our simulated data, these outcomes are stored directly as continuous per-lot-per-year values.

In the study, the health survey was conducted biennially while our simulated data contains annual values for this information.

In terms of selecting the controls, there were initially two groups of randomly selected control lots as seen in Figure 1. The first pool limited eligible vacant lots to only those that had at least 1 open code violation. This control group was selected for the study's final findings as these were a better statistical match to greened lots (in terms of area, age, and unemployment).

```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: "center"
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-alt: "Alt text here"
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("images", "figure1.png"))
```

The pre-greening period for each treated lot was defined as the years prior to the year that the lot was greened/treated (from 1999 to 2008), making a time-varying treatment status variable. This same pre-period was assigned to the 3 randomly selected, matched control lots. The mean pre-treatment period for the vacant lots in the study was 6.18 years.

#### Sample size, time period, and geographic scope

The study covered 10 years, from 1999–2008. There were 17,744 total lots in this study, where 4,436 were treated and 13,308 were the associated matched controls (a 1:3 ratio).

---------------------------------------

### 2. Annotated Replication

#### **Regression Model**
::: {style="font-size: 0.85em;"}
$$Y_{it}=\beta_{0}+\beta_1P_{it}+\beta_2R_{it}+\beta_3(P_{it}\times R_{it})+\beta_{4}(S_{it}\times t)+ \beta_{5}(S_{it}\times M_{i})+\displaystyle\sum_{k=6}^{p}\beta_{k}X_{it}+\xi_{i}+\varepsilon_{it}$$
:::

Where each regression model included different health or safety outcome of interest, $Yit$. $it$ is a unit of observation, or lot x year combination. The most key term here for our difference-in-differences analysis is $\beta_{3}(P_{it}\times R_{it})$, which captures the additional change in outcomes for treated lots after greening relative to the pre-to-post change in control lots.

Before replicating this model, we need to take a deeper look at our data. 

#### How well is our data matched?
0 = control lots, 1 = treatment lots

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Lot-level average outcome during pre-greening period
sim_data <- sim_data %>%
  group_by(lot_id) %>%
  mutate(pre_mean = mean(crime_gun_assaults[post == 0])) %>%
  mutate(rel_year = year - greening_year) %>% 
  ungroup() 

baseline_lot <- sim_data %>%
  filter(post == 0) %>%
  group_by(lot_id) %>%
  slice_min(year, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup()

baseline_lot %>%
  select(treated, area_sqft, median_age, unemployed_sqmi,
         college_sqmi, income_sqmi, black_sqmi, hispanic_sqmi, 
         poverty_sqmi,vacant_lot_density) %>%
  tbl_summary(by = treated, statistic = all_continuous() ~ "{mean} ({sd})")
```

One of the key assumptions of a difference-in-differences analysis is parallel trends between the treatment and control groups during the pre-treatment period. This allows us to have a true causal estimate of any effect that greening vacant lots have on the various health and safety outcomes. 

Let's take a look at the trends throughout the study period our different outcome variables!

#### Parallel trends assumption

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
# 1) long format
did_long <- sim_data %>%
  select(year, treated, greening_year,
         crime_gun_assaults, crime_robberies, crime_vandalism,
         crime_disorderly, crime_illegal_dumping,
         health_high_stress, health_high_chol, health_high_bp,
         health_low_exercise, health_poor_health) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -c(year, treated, greening_year),
               names_to = "outcome", values_to = "value")

# 2) means by year × treated within outcome
did_means <- did_long %>%
  group_by(outcome, year, treated) %>%
  summarize(mean_value = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# 3) define a reference "treatment year" line (average greening year among treated)
treat_year <- sim_data %>%
  filter(treated == 1) %>%
  summarize(avg_year = round(mean(greening_year, na.rm = TRUE))) %>%
  pull(avg_year)

# 4) rename the plot & graph
facet_names <- c("crime_disorderly" = "Disorderly Conduct",
                 "crime_gun_assaults" = "Gun Assaults",
                 "crime_illegal_dumping" = "Illegal Dumping",
                 "crime_robberies" = "Robberies",
                 "crime_vandalism" = "Vandalism",
                 "health_high_bp" = "High Blood Pressure",
                 "health_high_chol" = "High Cholesterol",
                 "health_high_stress" = "High Stress",
                 "health_low_exercise" = "Low Exercise",
                 "health_poor_health" = "Poor General Health")

ggplot(did_means, aes(x = year, y = mean_value, color = factor(treated))) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 1.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = treat_year, linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("0" = "#6F4E37", "1" = "#50C878"),
                   labels = c("0" = "Control", "1" = "Treated")) +
  facet_wrap(~outcome, scales = "free_y",
             labeller = as_labeller(facet_names)) +
  labs(color = "Group",
       y = "Mean outcome",
       title = "Difference-in-Differences across outcomes",
       subtitle = "Dashed line = average greening year among treated lots") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.ticks.x = element_blank(),
        axis.text.x = element_blank())
```

It appears that some outcome variables meet the parallel trends assumption better than others, so we will select one health and one safety outcome that appears to have the best parallel trends pre-treatment. 

#### Selected outcomes for models
(insert close up image of 2 plots here!) 

Now that we have analyzed the parallel trends assumption for our outcome variables, gun assaults and high cholesterol, let's run some models!

#### Regression to estimate treatment effect

Let's use the `fixest` package to run a model with i(), a syntax for including a period, treatment, and reference in a DiD. 

Here, i(rel_year, treated_status, ref = -1) creates an interaction between treatment status and each year relative to greening, producing a separate coefficient for each year. The year before greening (rel_year = -1) is excluded as the reference period.

Let's do our safety outcome first.

```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Create model based on equation
model1 <- feols(
  crime_gun_assaults ~ i(rel_year, treated_status, ref = -1) + # B1, B2, B3
                       section:pre_mean + # B5
                       area_sqft + vacant_lot_density + # lot covariates
                       unemployed_sqmi + college_sqmi + # Baseline covariates
                       income_sqmi + black_sqmi +
                       hispanic_sqmi + poverty_sqmi + median_age |
                       section^year, # B4 - dummy variables section x yr 
  data = sim_data,
  cluster = ~ contig_group_id) # cluster ID

iplot(model1, xlab = "Greening",
      ylab = "Gun Assaults",
      main = "Event Study: Gun Assaults")
```
This plot supports the parallel trends assumption, showing similar pre-treatment trends between treated and control lots before greening, followed by a clear reduction in gun assaults immediately after treatment.

Next, let's look at our health outcome. 

```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Create model based on equation
model2 <- feols(
  health_high_chol ~ i(rel_year, treated_status, ref = -1) + # B1, B2, B3
                       section:pre_mean + # B5
                       area_sqft + vacant_lot_density + # lot covariates
                       unemployed_sqmi + college_sqmi + # Baseline covariates 
                       income_sqmi + black_sqmi +
                       hispanic_sqmi + poverty_sqmi + median_age |
                       section^year, # B4 - dummy variables section x yr 
  data = sim_data,
  cluster = ~ contig_group_id) # cluster ID

iplot(model2, xlab = "Greening",
      ylab = "High Cholestrol",
      main = "Event Study: High Cholestrol")
```
This plot shows a slightly less consistent trend pre-treatment and a less clear reduction in high cholesterol post-treatment. 

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
# Lot-level average outcome during pre-greening period
sim_data <- sim_data %>% 
  group_by(lot_id) %>% 
  mutate(pre_mean = mean(crime_gun_assaults[post == 0])) %>% 
  ungroup() 
```

Now, let's run some models just using an interaction term (post:treated) and look at our key term from the study's model, $\beta_{3}(P_{it}\times R_{it})$, as this captures the additional change in outcomes for treated lots after greening relative to the pre-to-post change in control lots.

```{r}
# Create model based on equation- selected safety outcome
safety_model <- feols(
  crime_gun_assaults ~ post + treated_status + post:treated_status +     # B1, B2, B3
                       section:pre_mean +   # B5
                       area_sqft + vacant_lot_density +    # lot covariates
                       unemployed_sqmi + college_sqmi +    # demographics (baseline covariates)
                       income_sqmi + black_sqmi +
                       hispanic_sqmi + poverty_sqmi + median_age |
                       section^year,                        # B4- dummy variables section x yr 
  data = sim_data,
  cluster = ~ contig_group_id # cluster ID
)
```

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#summary(safety_model)

tidy(safety_model) |>
  filter(term == "post:treated_status") %>% 
    gt() |>
  fmt_number(decimals = 3) |>
  cols_label(
    term = "Term",
    estimate = "Estimate",
    std.error = "Std. Error",
    statistic = "t-value",
    p.value = "p-value"
  )
```

```{r}
# Create model based on equation- selected health outcome
health_model <- feols(
  health_high_chol ~ post + treated_status + post:treated_status +       # B1, B2, B3
                       section:pre_mean +   # B5
                       area_sqft + vacant_lot_density +    # lot covariates
                       unemployed_sqmi + college_sqmi +    # demographics (baseline covariates)
                       income_sqmi + black_sqmi +
                       hispanic_sqmi + poverty_sqmi + median_age |
                       section^year,                        # B4- dummy variables section x yr 
  data = sim_data,
  cluster = ~ contig_group_id # cluster ID
)
```

```{r}
#| code-fold: true
#summary(health_model)

tidy(health_model) |>
  filter(term == "post:treated_status") |> 
    gt() |>
  fmt_number(decimals = 3) |>
  cols_label(
    term = "Term",
    estimate = "Estimate",
    std.error = "Std. Error",
    statistic = "t-value",
    p.value = "p-value"
  )
```

Describe similarities and differences in model output here!! overall, by geographic sector, etc.
---------------------------------------

### 3. Critical Evaluation

After running our models, we will reflect on our findings in the context of the study design. 

#### Causal identification: Is the identification strategy credible? What would have to be true for the estimates to be causal?

The study and its replication indicate that the causal relationship appears reasonably credible overall. However, some measurements show less parallelism and lack significant statistical relevance in the post-treatment phase.

The author also breaks down the analysis by regions in Philadelphia, revealing that some areas and categories seem to be more effective than others. This likely reflects local characteristics of the urban environment, such as neighborhood structure, density, and how residents actually use the greened spaces.

In the results, crime outcomes appear stronger and more consistent, while health outcomes are relatively weaker. This makes sense, as health is influenced by many factors beyond the intervention. The study suggests that greening can clearly reduce crime, while the evidence for health improvements is less compelling.

Some outcomes are more challenging to interpret. For instance, reports of disorderly incidents increase before treatment and even more after treatment. One possibility is that greening attracts more people to the area, leading to increased foot traffic and consequently more reported disorder, even if serious crime decreases. Meanwhile, outcomes related to low exercise seem to show more improvement.

#### Statistical assumptions: Are key assumptions tested or defended? Do you find the evidence convincing?

We observed that some pre-trends are slightly off, particularly in areas such as vandalism, disorderly conduct, and poor health.

However, Our replicated trends mostly show parallel patterns for gun assaults, cholesterol levels, and several other outcomes. The event-study replication assesses the parallel trends assumption by checking whether treated and control areas had similar trends before the treatment took place.

Overall, the evidence supporting the statistical assumptions is generally convincing, for them mostly fits the assumptions of parallel trends.

#### Sampling & external validity: Who is in the sample? To whom can results be generalized?

The sample consists of vacant lots in Philadelphia that were either treated with a greening intervention or used as control sites.

These results might be applicable to similar greening programs. Urban contexts with similar vacancy patterns could provide helpful insights, but we believe it is challenging to generalize the results beyond these types of cities.

While there are some indications of effects, such as a reduction in gun violence and improvements in cholesterol and blood pressure, at 95% of the confidence intervals for the estimated treatment effects across all health outcomes cross zero. Additionally, the findings related to crime are somewhat mixed.

This suggests that while there may be some positive effects, the findings are not definitive and should be interpreted cautiously.

#### Measurement: Are the outcome and treatment variables measured accurately and without systematic bias?

Crime outcomes are likely measured relatively consistently because they rely on official crime reports. However, some outcomes such as vandalism and disorderly incidents may also reflect increased activity in the area. If greening attracts more people to use the space, there could be more opportunities for these events to occur or to be reported. There may also be some reporting bias depending on frequency of activities.

However, we think that health outcomes are more difficult to interpret. The study itself discusses that health effects are weaker and harder to detect. It is difficult to clearly link greening directly to health outcomes because many other factors affect health.

For example, self-reported poor health and high stress is a relatively weak measure. Blood pressure and cholesterol are more biologically direct indicators, but they can still be affected by many external influences. These outcomes are still influenced by factors such as diet, lifestyle, and access to healthcare, making it harder to attribute changes directly to the greening intervention.

#### Other limitations: Anything else that affects how much weight you would put on the study’s conclusions?

The separation of results by regions in Philadelphia still seems spectacle to us. While it makes sense to some extent since neighborhood context could influence how greening interventions are effective, the models already account for socioeconomic factors such as income and demographic variables. Therefore, it's not entirely clear how much additional insight the regional split provides.

It feels somewhat like a stretch to interpret the data this way, although it's still plausible that neighborhood context affects the effectiveness of the interventions.

Overall, the study offers evidence that greening vacant lots can reduce certain types of crime and improve health to some extent. However, the evidence regarding health outcomes is weaker, and conclusions should be drawn carefully. We believe that has to do with behavioural choices of health and lifestyle. Because of these factors, while the findings are suggestive and interesting, they should still be interpreted with caution.
